title: The Attendance Gap: Why a Full Membership Book Doesn't Mean a Profitable Winter for Fitness Studios
linkedin:
A fitness studio's July revenue often looks identical to its January revenue. The room does not.

Direct debit billing has no way to tell the difference between a member training four times a week and one who has not tapped in since Easter. Both show up as the same paying member on the same report.

That gap between what is billed and what is actually used is the pattern we see most often when we sit down with fitness and wellness operators mid year. It is not a marketing problem and it is barely a weather problem.

It is a timing problem. Members who have quietly disengaged rarely cancel straight away, the direct debit keeps running for months out of inertia or an unfulfilled intention to get back into it.

The cancellation wave that eventually lands through spring is really just the formal recognition of a decision made, in practice, months earlier during a quiet winter nobody was watching closely.

The studios that get ahead of it are tracking real session attendance against the roster, not just membership count against total staff. A 6am class can run half empty while the 5:30pm slot everyone wants is capped by a headcount set back in January.

Attendance, not billing, is the earliest honest signal of where a membership base actually sits. It is also the number least likely to get reviewed with any real discipline.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#SMEFinance #BusinessOwners #ProfitabilityAdvice
facebook:
Ever noticed a gym or studio's booking sheet looking full through winter, but the actual room feeling half empty?

That gap is more common than most owners realise. Direct debit billing keeps running long after someone has quietly stopped showing up, so the revenue number looks steady while real attendance has already moved on.

The catch is that the eventual wave of cancellations usually lands months later, in spring, once people finally act on a decision they made back in the middle of winter.

Anyone running a membership based business noticed this pattern in their own numbers?

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for East Coast studio and gym owners heading through the winter months: if membership revenue looks steady but the room feels quieter than usual, it is worth checking whether billing and attendance have quietly drifted apart.

Direct debit revenue keeps running the same whether a member trains four times a week or has not shown up since Easter, so a steady revenue report can hide a membership base that is losing engagement well before anyone formally cancels. That eventual wave of cancellations tends to land months later, through spring, once people act on a decision that was really made much earlier.

Studios and gyms across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria that check real session attendance against their roster through winter, rather than relying on membership numbers alone, usually find one or two time slots quietly carrying most of the cost for very little value, and get months of lead time to fix it before it shows up as a lost member.

Read the full piece on the blog.
